title: Trauma and the Body's Knowing: Trusting Somatic Intelligence in Daily Life
excerpt: Discover how trauma shapes bodily responses and learn to trust your body's somatic intelligence for healing and resilience every day.
body:
Understanding Somatic Intelligence: More Than Just a Feeling

When trauma occurs, it imprints itself not only on our minds but profoundly on our bodies. Somatic intelligence refers to the body's intrinsic ability to sense, interpret, and respond to internal and external stimuli. This form of intelligence is nonverbal and often unconscious, yet it is a critical guide in understanding how trauma lives within us.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneering trauma researcher, famously noted in The Body Keeps the Score that trauma is stored in the body, not just in memory. This storage manifests as physiological dysregulation, chronic tension, or sudden emotional reactions that can seem disconnected from the present moment. Understanding and trusting somatic intelligence means tuning into these bodily messages rather than dismissing or suppressing them.

Why the Body Remembers: Trauma’s Somatic Footprint

Trauma isn't just an event that happened in the past—it's a lived experience etched into the nervous system. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma disrupts the body’s natural ability to complete self-protective responses, leading to a state of chronic dysregulation.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory further clarifies how trauma impacts the autonomic nervous system. The body's safety cues become distorted, leading to states of fight, flight, freeze, or shutdown that may activate inappropriately in daily life. The body’s somatic intelligence tries to signal these states through sensations such as tightness in the chest, stomach knots, or a racing heart.

Recognizing Somatic Signals in Daily Life

For many, these sensations are confusing or frightening, especially when they appear without obvious triggers. Yet, they are vital data points from your body’s nervous system, signaling what it needs for regulation and safety.

  • Notice patterns: Pay attention to recurrent sensations or emotional states linked to particular situations or people.
  • Track physiological shifts: Heart rate changes, muscle tension, or breathing irregularities are clues to underlying trauma responses.
  • Label the experience: Naming sensations (e.g., “I feel tightness in my jaw”) helps create a mindful distance and reduces overwhelm.

Deb Dana, a clinician and educator on Polyvagal Theory, suggests that this simple practice of attuning to somatic signals is a first step toward nervous system regulation.

Trusting the Body: Moving Beyond Intellectual Understanding

Many people stuck in therapy feel intellectually aware of their trauma yet remain disconnected from their bodies. Trusting somatic intelligence requires shifting from cognitive control to embodied awareness.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, highlights that somatic awareness allows trauma survivors to reclaim a sense of agency. This means learning to respond to bodily cues with curiosity instead of avoidance, which can gradually rewire the nervous system for safety.

“The body is the unconscious mind — it knows what words can’t express.” — Peter Levine

Practical Tools to Engage Somatic Intelligence Daily

Integrating somatic awareness into everyday life doesn’t require formal therapy sessions. Here are accessible strategies supported by somatic trauma research:

  • Body scans: Briefly check in with different body areas multiple times a day to notice tension or discomfort.
  • Mindful breathing: Slow, diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system and promotes regulation.
  • Movement practices: Gentle yoga, walking, or stretching help discharge stored tension and reconnect you with bodily sensations.
  • Grounding techniques: Feeling your feet on the floor or holding a textured object can anchor you in the present.

These tools are grounded in the work of Dan Siegel and Gabor Maté, who emphasize the importance of integrating mind and body for trauma recovery.

When Somatic Intelligence Feels Overwhelming

Sometimes tuning into the body can initially increase distress, especially if traumatic memories or sensations feel unbearable. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy demonstrates that gradual, titrated exposure to traumatic material paired with bilateral stimulation can help regulate overwhelming somatic responses.

It’s important to approach somatic awareness with patience and, if needed, the support of a trauma-informed practitioner. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model also supports working compassionately with different parts of the self, including those holding trauma in the body.

Building a Somatic Relationship for Long-Term Healing

Healing trauma is not about erasing the past but about cultivating a new relationship with your body’s intelligence. This relationship is one of trust, curiosity, and compassion, allowing you to navigate life with increased resilience.

Regular somatic practice rewires the nervous system toward safety and integration, as shown by decades of neurobiological trauma research. When you listen and respond to your body’s signals, you reclaim presence and agency that trauma once stole.

In the words of Gabor Maté, "The question is not why the addiction, but why the pain." Similarly, the question for trauma recovery is not why the symptoms, but what the body is trying to communicate.

Conclusion: Embodied Wisdom as a Path Forward

Living with trauma is living with a body that remembers. Somatic intelligence is your body’s innate wisdom, offering a roadmap out of chronic dysregulation and disconnection. By trusting and engaging this intelligence daily, you reclaim your body, your mind, and your life.

For those who have tried talk therapy and still feel stuck, somatic work offers a grounded, accessible path to healing that honors the whole person—not just the mind. Integrating the science of somatic trauma recovery with daily practices can transform your relationship with yourself, one sensation at a time.

References:

  • Bessel van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score, 2014.
  • Peter Levine, Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma, 1997.
  • Stephen Porges, The Polyvagal Theory, 2011.
  • Deb Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.
  • Pat Ogden, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, 2006.
  • Gabor Maté, In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts, 2008.
  • Dan Siegel, The Developing Mind, 1999.
  • Francine Shapiro, EMDR Therapy, 2018.
  • Richard Schwartz, Internal Family Systems Therapy, 1995.

faqs.a: Somatic intelligence is the body's ability to sense, interpret, and respond to physical and emotional cues, especially those related to trauma. It operates largely outside of conscious awareness and guides our internal regulation.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the body?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts the nervous system’s natural regulation, causing chronic states of fight, flight, freeze, or shutdown. These states manifest as physical sensations and emotional responses stored in the body long after the traumatic event.

faqs.q: Can I access somatic intelligence without therapy?
faqs.a: Yes. Practices like body scanning, mindful breathing, movement, and grounding can help you tune into your body's signals. However, some trauma responses may require professional support to navigate safely.

faqs.q: Why do bodily sensations sometimes feel overwhelming during somatic work?
faqs.a: Trauma can create strong, stored sensations that may feel intense when accessed. This is normal, and gradual, compassionate engagement—sometimes with professional guidance—helps build tolerance and regulation.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic approaches can aid healing.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces Somatic Experiencing and practical methods for resolving trauma through body awareness.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: A clinical guide to understanding and applying Polyvagal Theory to regulate the nervous system.

metaTitle: Trauma & Somatic Intelligence: Trusting the Body Daily
metaDescription: Learn how trauma affects your body and how to trust your somatic intelligence for healing and resilience every day.
author: The Oracle Lover
